chapterSummary.title: Patterns Around Us
chapterSummary.content: This chapter helps students discover and understand the wonderful world of patterns. Students learn about two main types: repeating patterns (where a core group comes back again and again) and growing patterns (where each step changes by following a rule). The chapter uses everyday examples from Indian life -- rangoli designs, floor tiles, saree borders, and mehndi art -- to make patterns fun and relatable. Students also explore number sequences, learn to find the rule behind a pattern, and use that rule to predict what comes next.
keyPoints: A repeating pattern has a core that comes back in the same order again and again
A growing pattern changes at each step, usually by getting bigger
Number sequences follow rules like 'add 3' or 'subtract 5'
Finding the difference between consecutive numbers helps discover the rule
Multiplication tables are number patterns (e.g., table of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16...)
Patterns appear in nature, art, music, and everyday objects
learningObjectives: Identify and extend repeating patterns using shapes, colours, and objects
Find the core of a repeating pattern
Recognise growing patterns and describe how they change
Discover the rule behind a number sequence
Predict the next terms in a pattern using the rule
Create original patterns using shapes and numbers

curatedNotes.title: Two Types of Patterns
curatedNotes.content: Repeating patterns have a core that keeps coming back. Example: red, blue, blue, red, blue, blue -- the core is 'red, blue, blue'. Growing patterns change at each step. Example: 1, 3, 5, 7 -- the pattern grows by adding 2. To tell the difference, ask yourself: does the same group come back, or does each step change?

curatedNotes.title: How to Find the Rule
curatedNotes.content: Step 1: Write down the numbers in the pattern. Step 2: Subtract each number from the next one. Step 3: If all the differences are the same, that number is your rule. Example: 6, 12, 18, 24. Differences: 12-6=6, 18-12=6, 24-18=6. Rule: add 6. For decreasing patterns like 40, 35, 30, 25: differences are all 5, so the rule is subtract 5.

curatedNotes.title: Patterns Are Everywhere!
curatedNotes.content: Look around you for patterns! The tiles on your floor, the bricks in a wall, the design on a churidar, the petals on a flower, the stripes on a flag -- all have patterns. Even time follows a pattern: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday... repeats every week. Music has rhythm patterns too. The more you look, the more patterns you will find!
